coredata.dc:title: Effective photodegradation of rhodamine B and levofloxacin over CQDs modified BiOCl and BiOBr composite: Mechanism and toxicity assessment
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yanyan
Guo, Hongxia
Liu, Jie
Xia, Qiong
Liu, Jifang
Liang, Xuhua
Liu, Enzhou
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description:
In this contribution, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 3D-flower like BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) photocatalyst were successfully prepared via a facile mechanical compounding method. The crystal structure, surface composition, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic activities were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activity of the as-obtained photocatalyst were evaluated by degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and Levofloxacin (LEV) under near IR-UV–vis light irradiation, the CQDs/BiOX composite displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity as compared with individual BiOX materials. The CQDs/BiOX composite had the outstanding light harvesting and electron transfer ability because of the ordered ultrathin nanosheet structure of the BiOX, the formation of metal Bi under photoinduction, and the synergistic effects between CQDs and pure BiOX. Antibacterial activity and effects on Rye seeds growth of the LEV degradation intermediate were also researched. Reactive-species-trapping experiments exhibited that h+ and
O2 − were the active reactive species during photodegradation process. This work provided an effective and simple strategy for designing QDs modified Bi‐rich oxyhalides in organic pollutant containing wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Bismuth oxyhalides
Photodegradation
Toxicity experiments
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The biology and environment toxicities of materials
The biology and environment toxicities of degradation intermediate on Rye seed growth
Crystal structure and surface composition of the as-prepared samples
The BET, UV–vis DRS spectroscopy, PL spectra, photocurrent vs. time (I-t) curves, and the EIS curves the of the as-prepared samples
The biology and environment toxicities of photocatalyst
Possible RhB photodegradation pathway and toxicity analysis of RhB degradation intermediates
Photodegradation of LEV and possible degradation pathway of LEV
Potential roles of reactive species in LEV degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b), (c) HRTEM images, (d) PL spectrum, (e) UV–vis spectrum, and (f) FTIR spectra of the as-prepred CQDs.
XRD pattern of (a) BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl, (b) BiOBr and CQDs/BiOBr, and (c) BiOI. (d) FTIR spectra of the as-prepared samples.
XPS spectra of the as-prepared samples, (a) full-scan spectra, high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, (d) Bi 4f, (e) Cl 2p, (f) Br 3d, and (g) I 3d.

SEM images of (a) BiOBr, (b) BiOCl, (c) BiOI, (d) CQDs/BiOCl, (g) CQDs/BiOBr. EDS spectrum of (e) CQDs/BiOCl, and (h) CQDs/BiOBr. Elemental mapping of (f) CQDs/BiOCl, (i) CQDs/BiOBr. TEM and HRTEM images of (j-l) CQDs/BiOCl, and (m-o) CQDs/BiOBr.
(a) UV–vis spectrum, (b) Time-dependent-PL spectrum of RhB over CQDs/BiOBr composite; (c) Adsorption performance, and (d) Photodegradation rate of the as-prepared samples.
The growth state of Rye seeds in (a) deionized water, (d) RhB solution with the concentration of 10 mg·L−1, RhB solution photodegraded with CQDs/BiOBr (c) at 30 min and (d) at 60 min.
(a) UV spectrum, (b) Time-dependent-PL spectrum, (c) HPLC chromatography, and (d) Photodegradation rates of LEV with the as-prepared samples.
(a) Antibacterial activity towards E. coli over untreated and treated LEV, (b) The growth state of Rye seeds in untreated and treated LEV solution.
UV spectrum of NBT transformation, (a) BiOCl, (b) BiOBr, (c) BiOI, (d) CQDs/BiOCl, (e) CQDs/BiOBr, and (f) All the as-prepared samples.
PL spectra of TAOH, (a) BiOCl, (b) BiOBr, (c) BiOI, (d) CQDs/BiOCl, (e) CQDs/BiOBr, and (f) All the as-prepared samples.

(a) Effects of different radical scavengers over the as-prepared samples. DMPO spin trapping EPR spectra for DMPO-
O2 − of (b) CQDs/BiOBr, and (c) CQDs/BiOCl composite.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic process of the as-prepared samples, (a) CQDs, (b) BiOX, CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr samples.
Schematic illustration of the constitution and charge transfer over CQDs/BiOX composite.

head.ce:title: Effective photodegradation of rhodamine B and levofloxacin over CQDs modified BiOCl and BiOBr composite: Mechanism and toxicity assessment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this contribution, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 3D-flower like BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) photocatalyst were successfully prepared via a facile mechanical compounding method. The crystal structure, surface composition, morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic activities were investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activity of the as-obtained photocatalyst were evaluated by degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) and Levofloxacin (LEV) under near IR-UV–vis light irradiation, the CQDs/BiOX composite displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity as compared with individual BiOX materials. The CQDs/BiOX composite had the outstanding light harvesting and electron transfer ability because of the ordered ultrathin nanosheet structure of the BiOX, the formation of metal Bi under photoinduction, and the synergistic effects between CQDs and pure BiOX. Antibacterial activity and effects on Rye seeds growth of the LEV degradation intermediate were also researched. Reactive-species-trapping experiments exhibited that h+ and
O2 − were the active reactive species during photodegradation process. This work provided an effective and simple strategy for designing QDs modified Bi‐rich oxyhalides in organic pollutant containing wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, A large number of researchers focus their studies on the high ecological risks of antibiotic contamination [1,2]. Levofloxacin (LEV) is a typical third-generation fluoroquinolone antibacterial agent with broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, has been widely applied in healthcare industry, animal husbandry and agricultural. The release and accumulation of LEV in water environments have leaded a number of environmental problems, such as bring a series of nonnegligible harm to human water security and waterbody biodiversity [3–5]. Therefore, an efficient, green, eco-friendly, and sustainable technology should be employed to figure out the water environmental pollution.

Photocatalytic technology is regarded as a promising strategy for antibiotic-containing wastewater treatment [6–12]. Among massive explored photocatalysts, bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = Cl, Br, I) have got a lot of attentions due to their outstanding oxidation performance, good physical and chemical stability, environmental benignity, as well as the special anisotropic sandwiched structure [13,14]. A [Bi2O2]2+ layer of BiOX is sandwiched by two X− layers in the crystal, the static electric field perpendicular to [Bi2O2]2+ layer and X− layers can facilitate the separation of photogenerated carriers, contributing to a good photocatalytic activity [15]. The band gap of BiOCl is about 3.2∼3.6 eV, which can response UV light effectively [16,17]. BiOBr and BiOI are the active visible-light-driven photocatalyst, their band gap are about 2.6∼2.9 eV and 1.63∼1.94 eV [18,19], respectively. Nowadays, BiOX have been extensively employed in water pollution treatment. For example, BiOCl with oxygen vacancy (OV-BOC) had the excellent photocatalytic activity for organic pollutants degradation [20]. 3D flower-like BiOBr composed of good-ordered nanoplates could degrade cefixime to CO2, H2O and some small molecules [21]. BiOI with narrow band gap and low valence band was employed as a promising visible-light-driven photocatalyst for phenol degradation [22]. However, poor light harvesting ability, slow carrier transfer and separation rate, and low e−-h+ separation capability of individual BiOX has seriously hindered their practical applications. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a new strategy to improve the light response ability, promote carrier formation and separation rate, finally to enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOX.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with outstanding biocompatibility, non-toxic, and good dispersibility in water [23–25], has been identified as a promising material to modify BiOX. There are three major reasons for using CQDs as a cocatalyst to boom the photocatalytic ability of BiOX: (i) CQDs can reduce interfacial defects and facilitate carrier transport and separation [26], (ii) CQDs can enhance the light absorption properties of photocatalyst [27], (iii) Small size effect of CQDs can provide more active sites to promote catalytic reaction process [28]. While, CQDs/BiOX composite had been reported by some researchers, and they confirmed that the modification of CQDs can effectively enhance the concentration and the transfer rate of the photogenerated carriers, which were mainly attributed to the superior electron transfer ability and enhanced light harvesting [24,29–33]. Summary of some reported preparation methods and photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOX were displayed in Table. S1.

In this report, 3D flower-like BiOX photocatalyst consisting of many ultra-thin nanosheets were prepared through a facile low temperature coprecipitation method. According to the diffusion formula of t = d2/k2D (here d, k and D is the particle size, the constant and the diffusion coefficient of electron-hole pairs, respectively), reduce d values can enlarge D values [34,35]. Therefore, ultrathin nanosheet structure of BiOX can improve the separation efficiency of e−-h+ pairs. The CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite were synthesized through a mechanical compounding method. This method is green, environmental protection and energy saving compared to the published literatures cited in Table. S1. The crystal structure, surface composition, morphology, microstructure, and optical properties of the as-prepared samples were investigated in detail. The CQDs/BiOX photocatalyst exhibited a remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity for LEV photodegradation. Especially, environmental toxicity experiments employing Rye seeds and E. coli were investigated. This work offers a new insight for preparing of novel photocatalyst in organic pollutant containing wastewater treatment.

In this report, CQDs have been prepared from the forsythia leaves through a hydrothermal method. 0.6 g of the forsythia leaves power and 70 mL of deionized water were stirred under pH = 3 for 30 min, after that the mixture were placed into a 100 mL teflon-lined autoclave with heating 200 °C for 8 h. The CQDs solution were obtained via a centrifugal process subsequently, and the synthesis schematic illustration was shown in Scheme 1 (a).

The individual BiOX (X = Cl, Br, I) photocatalyst were prepared by a facile low temperature coprecipitation process. 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 1 mmol of KX (X = Cl, Br, I) were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water for stirring 60 min to get a precipitated substance. After a centrifuging and drying process, the BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI photocatalyst were obtained.

The CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite were prepared as follows: 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 1 mmol of KX (X = Cl, Br) were dissolved in 70 mL of deionized water for stirring 60 min at room temperature. After that, 30 mL of the as-prepared CQDs solution were added into above mixture for stirring 30 min, the CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite were obtained after a centrifuging and a drying process, and the synthesis process is shown in Scheme 1 (b).
The characterization details of the as-prepared samples were shown in supplementary material.

Photocatalytic activities of the as-prepared samples were researched for photodegradation of RhB and LEV under IR-UV–visible light irradiation. Briefly, 100 mL of the RhB/LEV solution (10 mg·L−1) containing 50 mg of the photocatalyst stirred in the dark for 50 min to get an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. During the photodegradation process, the mixture was exposed to the IR-UV–visible light irradiation and 5 mL of the suspension was withdrawn at given time intervals. The concentrations of pollutants were detected via UV–vis spectrophotometer and HPLC method. Photodegradation rate was calculated by Eq.
(1) . Where C0 and Ct were the initial and reaction concentration, respectively.
(1)
Photodegradation
r a t e % = ( 1 -
C t
C 0
) × 100 %

The stability experiments of the as-prepared samples were carried out as follows: The used photocatalyst were collected by a centrifugation and water washing process for 3 times, then dried over night at 60 °C. The collected photocatalyst were used to degrade LEV to evaluate the stability of the as-prepared samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biology and environment toxicities of materials
ce:section.ce:para: The biology and environment toxicities of materials were studied. Here, Rye seeds were washed with deionized water for 2 h, then soaked in 0.5% KMnO₄ solution for 20 min, after that the Rye seeds were washed with sterile water until no KMnO₄. The seeds of the same size were immersed in a petri dish containing 200 μL of water and containing 10 mg of the as-prepared samples as blank control group and experimental group, respectively. The petri dishes were put in a dark environment at 25 °C. After 7 days, the stem length was measured to determine the biology and environment toxicities of materials on plant growth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biology and environment toxicities of degradation intermediate on Rye seed growth
ce:section.ce:para: The seeds of the same size were immersed in a petri dish containing 200 μL of RhB or LEV solution (untreated RhB or LEV solution and degraded RhB or LEV solution at different time) in a dark environment at 25 °C, and 200 μL of aqueous solution was used as blank control experiment. After 7 days, the stem length was measured to determine the effect of degradation products on plant growth.

Antimicrobial activity test of LEV solution before and after photodegradation were performed using E. coli in aseptic conditions as follows: The E. coli cells solution (106 cfu/mL) were homogeneously plated on a Luria-Bertani (LB) solid medium, sterilized filter paper (8 mm in diameter) with 20 μL of the antibiotic intermediate aqueous solution were placed on this plate with incubating at 37 °C, the inhibition zones were measured to evaluate the antibacterial activity of the LEV intermediate.

Radical species capturing experiments were carried out to investigate the possible reaction mechanism. 1 mmol of ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 1 mmol of potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) were added in the LEV degradation process as h+ and e− trapping agent, respectively. Nitro blue tetrazolium chloride monohydrate (NBT, 0.05 mmol·L−1) was employed to detect superoxide radical (
O2 −) based on the maximum UV absorption peak of NBT was 259 nm, while the formazan precipitate had no absorption peak at 259 nm, and the chemical reaction was shown in Eq.
(2) . The TAOH photoluminescence method was applied to study the hydroxyl radical (
OH), and the reaction was shown in Eq.
(3) .
(2)
(3)

The as-synthesized CQDs was dark brown powder after centrifugation and dry at 60 ℃ (Fig. 1 a inset), and which exhibited a bright blue color at UV light irradiation (Fig. 1d inset). Fig. 1(a∼c) were the TEM and HRTEM images of the as-prepared CQDs. Well-dispersed CQDs with a fairy uniform size (an average diameter of 5∼10 nm) was observed. The lattice spacing of 0.212 nm was corresponding to the (100) plane of the graphene structure [36,37]. The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of CQDs were at about 360 nm and 445 nm (Fig. 1d), respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQDs in aqueous solution exhibited a broad absorption edge in the UV region (Fig. 1e), which may because of the various π-π* (C=C) and n-π* (C=O) transitions [38]. FTIR spectra of the CQDs was shown in Fig. 1f. The as-prepared CQDs contained rich surface groups, the signal at 3279, 2927, 2844, 1600, 1392, and 1039 cm−1 were the stretching vibration of -OH, -NH, -CH, bending vibration of -OH, carbonyl C=O, and -CN bond, respectively [39]. The structure characteristics results of CQDs were consistent with the reported literatures [24,25,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure and surface composition of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure of the as-prepared samples were detected by XRD pattern. In Fig. 2 a∼c, XRD diffraction peaks of the pure BiOCl, BiOBr and BiOI were in accordance with the standard card of JCPDS#06-0249, JCPDS#09-0393, and JCPDS#10-0445, respectively, the narrow and sharp signals indicated high crystallinity of the photocatalyst. For CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composites, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs were not observed because of the limited amount and low crystallinity of CQDs. FTIR spectra were displayed in Fig. 2d. The signals at 3439 and 1613 cm−1 were the typical stretching vibration and bending vibration of -OH [40], respectively. The peaks at 2921 and 2842 cm−1 were the -NH and -CH bonds [41], respectively. The peaks at 528 cm−1 were the Bi-O bond, but for pure BiOI, the Bi-O bond shifted to 487 cm−1 [21,36].

The surface composition and bonding energy of the as-prepared samples were studied by XPS spectra using C 1s peak at 284.6 eV as a reference [7,21]. The full-scan spectra revealed the consist of C 1s, Bi (4p, 4d, 4f, 5d), O (1s, 2s), Cl 2p, Br 3d, and I 3d (Fig. 3 a). In Fig. 3b, The bonding energy at 284.6 eV was C-C bond [42], the presence of C 1s in pure BiOX was the adventitious carbon from the instrument. In Fig. 3c, for CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr, O 1s peaks at 529.9 and 530.8 eV were the C-O and Bi-O bond, respectively [36,43]. For pure BiOI, the bonding energy shifted to 530.5 and 532.6 eV because of the existence of I−. In Fig. 3d, the two strong signals of CQDs/BiOCl composite at 158.7 and 163.9 eV were Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+ [36,38,40], respectively, while the peaks shifted to 158.3 and 163.7 eV for CQDs/BiOBr composite, which shifted to 158.8 and 164.1 eV for pure BiOI. The Cl 2p high-resolution XPS spectrum displayed one strong peak with binding energy at 198.24 eV (Fig. 3e). Fig. 3f showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of Br 3d, and the binding energy at 67.94 eV [44]. In Fig. 3g, the two peaks at 619.6 and 631.0 eV were the I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 [13,22], respectively. The results of XRD, FTIR and XPS suggested the successful preparation of the BiOX and CQDs/BiOX composite.

The morphologies and microstructures of the as-synthesized photocatalyst were characterized by SEM and TEM images. As displayed in Fig. 4 a∼c, pure BiOX photocatalyst with 3D flower like shape were assembled by plenty of ultrathin nanoflakes. The size of BiOCl and BiOBr were approximately 1∼8 μm, while the size of BiOI was bigger than BiOCl and BiOBr, which was about 2∼10 μm. Fig. 4d and Fig. 4g showed the typical SEM image of CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite, respectively. The morphologies of CQDs/BiOX composite were also 3D flower like shape with plenty of good-ordered ultrathin nanoplates, and their average size about 8 μm. The results of SEM-EDs (Fig. 4e and Fig. 4h) and elemental mapping measurements (Fig. 4f and Fig. 4i) revealed that C, O, Bi, X (Cl or Br) elements existed in CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite.

The microstructures of CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite were investigated by TEM images. As displayed in Fig. 4j∼o, numerous well-dispersed CQDs with the size of 5∼10 nm were uniformly dispersed on the surface of BiOCl and BiOBr nanosheets. The lattice spacings of 0.212, 0.272, and 0.276 nm could be attributed to the (100) plane of CQDs, (110) plane of BiOCl, and (110) plane of BiOBr, respectively. The results of SEM and TEM suggested the successful preparation of BiOX and CQDs/BiOX composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The BET, UV–vis DRS spectroscopy, PL spectra, photocurrent vs. time (I-t) curves, and the EIS curves the of the as-prepared samples
ce:section.ce:para: The specific surface area (BET) of the as-prepared BiOBr-x samples were investigated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and the results were displayed in Fig. S1. The BET of CQDs/BiOBr, BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl, BiOCl, and BiOI were about 50.027, 36.023, 40.703, 34.118 and 28.516 m2⋅g−1, respectively. The CQDs/BiOX composite exhibited larger surface areas, suggested higher adsorbability.

The optical absorption of the as-prepared samples were investigated via an UV–vis spectrometer. As shown in Fig. S2 (a-b), the absorption edge of BiOCl was about 370 nm, suggesting the only UV light response ability of BiOCl. After modified with CQDs, the CQDs/BiOCl composite exhibited the enhanced light absorption intensity. The absorption edge of BiOBr was about 450 nm, which can respond visible light effectively. Although the absorption edge of CQDs/BiOBr composite were not changed significantly, but the light absorption intensity was enhanced. Compared with the BiOCl, BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite, BiOI displayed a remarkably stronger absorption in the whole UV–vis spectra region. The bandgap energy (Eg) of BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI were about 3.29, 2.71, and 1.68 eV according to the plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hv, respectively. The results indicated that the introduce of CQDs could effectively enhance the light-harvest ability of BiOX photocatalyst.

The PL spectrums, photocurrent vs. time (I-t) curves and EIS curves were taken to study the generation, transport and separation capabilities of the photogenerated carriers over the as-prepared samples. Lower PL intensity, higher photocurrent density, and smaller arc radius of the photocatalyst suggested better transmission and separation ability of the photon-generated carriers [45,46]. As displayed in Fig. S2c∼e, CQDs/BiOBr displayed the lowest PL intensity, highest photocurrent density, and smallest arc radius, illustrated the excellent efficiency of e−-h+ separation ability and the outstanding photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/BiOBr composite.

Photodegradation of RhB with the as-prepared samples under IR-UV–vis light illumination were displayed in Fig. 5 . The peaks of RhB around 550 nm decreased obviously and the blue shift were observed during the photodegradation process (Fig. 5a), the emission peak of RhB was at 560 nm under 510 nm excitation wavelength, while the PL intensity were greatly decreased and the peaks were shifted to 528 nm during the photodegradation process (Fig. 5b). Under UV light irradiation, the RhB solution exhibited a bright orange color before degradation, which displayed a bright green color when the photodegradation time was 30 min, while the color was changed to blue after the photodegradation process (Fig. 5b inset). The results not only suggested the degradation of RhB, but also indicated the production of intermediates. The adsorption performance of the as-prepared samples were summarized in Fig. 5c, the adsorption quantity of RhB gradually increased before 20 min, after that the adsorption quantity did not increase significantly, suggesting the adsorption-desorption equilibrium were arrived after 20 min. Fig. 5d summarized the photodegradation rates of the as-prepared samples, the photodegradation rate of RhB was about 4.78% only under light illumination, suggesting the negligible of self-photolysis. The photodegradation rates of RhB with BiOCl, BiOBr, BiOI, CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr were about 92.26, 94.48, 86.41, 95.76 and 98.91% under 60 min light irradiation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The biology and environment toxicities of photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The biology and environment toxicities of photocatalyst towards Rye seeds were shown in Fig. S3. All the Rye seeds germinated both in water and in materials after 7 days cultivated at 25 °C, the average stalks were about 6.8, 5.0, 4.5, 3.8, 6.1, and 5.9 cm in water, BiOCl, BiOBr, BiOI, CQDs/BiOCl, and CQDs/BiOBr photocatalyst, respectively, which indicated the low biology and environment toxicities of the as-prepared photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible RhB photodegradation pathway and toxicity analysis of RhB degradation intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: HPLC-MS spectrometry were carried out to further investigate the degradation intermediates of RhB. The total ion chromatogram and corresponding mass spectra were shown in Fig. S4. Eighteen primary intermediates were detected, and the possible degradation pathway of RhB with CQDs/BiOBr composite might include one five steps: (i). Deethyl process of RhB; (ii). Chromophore cleavage process; (iii). Opening ring mineralization of big molecule intermediate; (IV). Several small molecules intermediate generated; (V). All of the intermediates were degraded to CO2, H2O, NH4 +, and NO3 −.

As diplayed in Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b, the shifted signals of RhB in UV–vis spectrum and in PL spectrum indicated the production of intermediates. The toxicity experiments were taken to evaluate the effects of RhB degradation intermediate on Rye seed growth and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . After 7 days cultivated at 25 °C, the germination and growth trend of Rye seeds in deionized water were good, the stalks were approximately 5∼6 cm (Fig. 6a), while the RhB and intermediate had a certain inhibitory effect on growth of Rye seeds (Fig. 6b and Fig. 6c). Notably, the germination and growth trend of Rye seeds cultured in RhB solution after photodegraded were optimal, the length of stalks were about 6∼7 cm (Fig. 6d), which may because of the existence of NH4 + and NO3 −. The results suggested the low environmental toxicity of RhB after photodegradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation of LEV and possible degradation pathway of LEV
ce:section.ce:para: Photodegradation of LEV were employed to further evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples. UV spectrum, time-dependent-PL spectrum, and HPLC chromatography were displayed in Fig. 7 a∼c, respectively. The peaks at 290 nm decreased along with the photodegradation time prolong (Fig. 7a), indicated the photodegradation of LEV. In Fig. 7b, Em peaks of LEV at 450 nm were greatly decreased and shifted to 510 nm as the photodegradation time prolong, while the LEV solution exhibited a bright aquamarine blue color before photodegradation process and exhibited a blue color after photodegradation process, indicating the production of intermediates. Fig. 7c displayed the HPLC chromatography of LEV, the intensity of the signals were decreased, illustrated the decompose of LEV. In Fig. 7d, the CQDs/BiOBr composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity for LEV degradation, and the order of the photocatalytic activity was that CQDs/BiOBr > CQDs/BiOCl > BiOBr > BiOCl > BiOI.

Possible degradation pathway of LEV with CQDs/BiOBr composite was revealed via a HPLC-MS spectrometry. According to the total ion chromatogram and corresponding mass spectra (Fig. S5 and Fig. S6), several important intermediates were detected, and the possible degradation pathways were displayed in Fig. 8 . LEV molecule could lose HCl to form C1, or may lose F to form C2. C1 and C2 could be degraded to C3∼C6, then continued to decompose to small molecules C7∼C10 as the degradation process prolong, finally all the intermediates could be degrade to NH4 +, NO3 −, CO2 and H2O with further oxidation/reactions process.

Cycling photocatalytic test were carried to evaluate the repeatability of the as-prepared photocatalyst due to the stability was an important factor for practical application [47,48]. From Fig. S7 we can clearly see that the degradation rates of LEV were not significantly decreased after three cycle experiments, indicating good repeatability of the as-prepared samples.

Antibacterial activity and effects on Rye seed growth of the LEV degradation intermediate were employed to further detect their environmental toxicity. Antibiotic activity towards E. coli with LEV intermediate were shown in Fig. 9 a and Table. S2. The untreated LEV solution had effective antibacterial activity, and the inhibitory zone diameter with 100 mg·L−1 of LEV was about 20 mm after cultivation at 37 °C for 12 h, the inhibition zone increased to 27 mm when culture at 37 °C for 24 h. The inhibition zone of 10 mg·L−1 of LEV was about 14 mm and 24 mm after cultivation at 37 °C for 12 h and 24 h, respectively. While, photodegradation LEV solution with narrowed inhibition zones were observed, suggested the weaker antibacterial activity, which may because of the decompose of LEV. The results indicated the LEV antibiotics were degraded and the degradation products had weak antibacterial activity.

Effects on Rye seed growth with untreated and treated LEV antibiotic solution were displayed in Fig. 9b. After 7 days of cultivation at 25 °C, the Rye seeds grew well in deionized water and the stalks were about 5 cm. The LEV solution had a certain inhibitory effect on the growth of Rye seeds, indicated a certain eco-environmental toxicity of LEV antibiotic. After photodegradation of LEV for 120 min, the germination rates of Rye seeds increased obviously, the stalks were about 3∼4 cm, which may because of the decompose of the LEV molecules. The results of toxicity analysis indicated the low environmental toxicity of LEV after photodegradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential roles of reactive species in LEV degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The generation of e−, h+, superoxide radical (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) were probed by K2Cr2O7, EDTA-2Na, NBT and TA. Fig. 10 (a-f) exhibited the spectrum of NBT transformation over the as-prepared samples. For BiOCl, BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl, and CQDs/BiOBr samples, the maximum absorption signals at 259 nm decreased obviously, suggested the major role of the
O2 −. While for BiOI (Fig. 10 c), the signals at 259 nm appeared negligible decrease, implying the minor role of the
O2 −. In Fig. 10 (f), the signal intensity at 259 nm of BiOBr was lower than that of the other photocatalyst, indicating the largest number of
O2 − were produced.

Fig. 11 (a-f) exhibited the PL spectra of TAOH over the as-prepared samples. Based on the Eq. (3), TA reacted with
OH to form TAOH, therefore, the strongest PL intensity of TAOH suggested the largest amount of
OH [49,50]. In Fig. 11 (a-e), the PL intensity of the TAOH over BiOCl, BiOBr, BiOI, CQDs/BiOCl, and CQDs/BiOBr increased as the photodegradation time prolong, indicating the generation of
OH. While in Fig. 11 (f), the intensity of the TAOH over BiOBr was stronger than that of the other photocatalyst, suggested the major role of the
OH over BiOBr during the photocatalytic process.

Effects of e− and h+ displayed in Fig. 12 (a). For BiOI photocatalyst, the LEV degradation rates were greatly decreased from 61.28% to 22.46% and 25.18% after addition of EDTA-2Na and K2Cr2O7 quenchers, suggested the major role of h+ and e−. For other photocatalyst, the degradation rates were obviously decreased after addition of EDTA-2Na while the degradation rates were no significant reduction after addition of K2Cr2O7, indicated the major role of h+ and minor role of e−.

As
O2 − active species were the major species for CQDs/BiOCl, and CQDs/BiOBr composite (Fig. 10), the electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR) was performed to further determine the role of
O2 −. Here, 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was worked as a free radical scavenger, and the results were shown in Fig. 12 (b) and (c). There were no
O2 − detected in the dark due to the weak signals in 0 min, while the strong signals were detected in 15 min and 30 min, suggested the generation of
O2 − under light irradiation.

Table. S3 summarized the roles of free radicals over the as-prepared samples. Based on the above experimental results, e− and h+ were the active reactive species for BiOI,
OH and
O2 − were the negative species for BiOBr, h+ and
O2 − were the major species for BiOCl, CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr.

The XPS valence band spectra were carried out to investigate the valence band (VB) of the as-prepared samples [51,52]. As shown in Fig. S8, the VB potentials of BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI were about 2.15, 2.41, and 2.04 V, respectively. The Eg of BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI were about 3.29, 2.71, and 1.68 eV according to the plots of (αhν)1/2 versus hv, respectively. As the conduction band (CB) potentials of the photocatalyst was equal to VB minus Eg (CB = VB-Eg), therefore, the CB potentials of BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI were about −1.14, −0.30, and 0.36 V, respectively.

According to the above results, possible mechanism over BiOX and CQDs/BiOX composite during photodegradation process were proposed in Fig. S9 and Scheme 2 . Under light irradiation, photogenerated electrons-holes pairs were generated in the CB and VB of the as-prepared samples, while the CQDs could convert near IR light to UV–vis light to enhance the light absorption properties of the as-prepared photocatalyst. Also, the ordered ultrathin nanosheet structure of BiOX could facilitate separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. As the redox potential of BiV/BiIII was about +1.59 V, the CB potentials of BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI were about −1.14, −0.30 and 0.36 V, which were negative than the redox potential of BiV/BiIII, therefore, the e− could firstly reduce Bi III to plasmonic Bi metal to form Bi/BiOX composite during the photocatalytic process [53]. e− could transfer from the CB of BiOCl and BiOBr to metal Bi under UV–Vis-near IR light irradiation. But for pure BiOI, the fermi level of metal Bi was more negative than the CB potential of BiOI (+0.36 V), indicated e− could transfer from metal Bi to the CB of BiOI (Fig. S9). For CQDs/BiOCl and CQDs/BiOBr composite, (i) CQDs would convert the near IR light to UV–vis light to improve light-harvesting ability; (ii) photogenerated electrons would transfer from the CB of BiOCl/BiOBr to metal Bi and CQDs to enhanced e−- h+ separation ability; (iii) Photoelectrons could active O2 to produce
O2 − for oxidative degradation; (iV) Photoproduction holes in the CB of BiOBr would active H2O to form
OH−, which may benefit to the photodegradation process; (V) All the active species would participate in the LEV degradation process to enhance the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/BiOX. Finally, LEV could be degraded to NH4 +, NO3 −, CO2 and H2O.

In summary, the individual 3D flower like BiOX photocatalyst were prepared via a facile low temperature coprecipitation process and the CQDs/BiOX (X = Cl, Br) composite were prepared via a mechanical adsorption process. The crystal structure and surface composition, morphologies, optical properties, as well as photocatalytic activities were investigated in detail. The CQDs/BiOBr displayed the excellent photocatalytic activity, and the improved photocatalytic activities of the CQDs/BiOX could be attribute to the ordered ultrathin nanosheet structure of BiOX, which could facilitate separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. As well as that the Bi III was reduced by photo-induced electron to form Bi/BiOX composite under light irradiation, which would facilitate electron transport. The possible RhB and LEV photodegradation pathways, toxicity analysis, and antibacterial activity of the LEV degradation intermediate were also researched. This work will open a new insight for designing QDs modified Bi‐rich oxyhalides to efficient removal of organic pollutants from waste water.

Yanyan Zhao: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Investigation. Hongxia Guo: Investigation. Jie Liu: Formal analysis. Qiong Xia: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jifang Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xuhua Liang: Investigation. Enzhou Liu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Jun Fan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261), the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (2021JQ-838), Natural Science Research project of Anhui Universities in 2021 (KJ2021A1181), and the Specialized Scientific Research Fund Projects of Academician Shengyong Zhang (20YSZX01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.07.046.

ce:section.ce:para:
